result:
There's something oddly comforting about the way old books smell—that musty, slightly sweet perfume of aging paper and ink that seems to transport you to another era even before you've read a single word. I've always thought libraries are among the most democratic places left in the world. Anyone can walk in, grab a seat among the stacks, and momentarily inhabit a life entirely unlike their own. You could be sitting next to someone grieving, someone escaping, someone studying for an exam that might change everything, or someone like me, just wandering through aisles because the world outside feels a little too loud.

I tried learning the guitar last summer. My fingers hurt constantly, and I sounded terrible, but there was a strange satisfaction in creating noise that slowly, almost imperceptibly, began to resemble music. Progress in anything creative is like watching ice melt—imperceptible in the moment but undeniable when you compare where you started to where you are now. The hardest part is showing up when you sound awful, when the gap between what you hear in your head and what comes out of your hands feels impossibly wide.

Coffee, I think, is the world's most socially acceptable way of admitting we need help functioning. We dress it up with oat milk and flavor shots, but at its core, it's a mild dependency that gets us through mornings. I'd be curious to know what future generations will think of our collective caffeine habit—will they see it as we now see Victorian-era tonics? Regardless, I'll be here, cup in hand, appreciating the small miracle of a fresh start contained in a warm mug.
